text:
8. The humans thought they were safe. Melusine’s mouth tugged into an involuntary smile when her scouts reported a ship finally putting out from the vast harbor where it had sat for twenty-one nights, along with all the others. She was surprised it had taken them so long to see the pattern in the attacks—or else they had seen it and simply hadn’t taken it seriously. Truth was, she had no idea what humans thought of them up there. Monsters, distant waterborne cousins, or nursery tales. By the time anyone faced the fact that the seafolk were real, it no longer mattered. No one came back to tell what had happened. On land they could only infer it from the ships that vanished before every third new moon, and from distant witnesses—enough to know the crewmen weren’t coming home.

Mel had her own opinion of humans, based on what she’d seen. Ignorant, forgetful. But not fools, and always progressing. Their ships kept getting faster, their weapons more precise and more solid, and not long ago, alongside harpoons, spears, and swords, thundering firearms had appeared—enough to kill more than one of her sisters. The hunt kept getting harder, but they were confident they could meet the humans head-on. As long as there was someone left to fight.

Neither the Queen nor the High Priestess had imagined that the greatest challenge of their reign would not be prey that resisted and advanced—forcing them to advance in turn—but prey that wasn’t there at all. In the lead-up to the third new moon the sea went quiet. Ships stopped leaving the harbors. At first, word came only from the North that victims were scarce, but soon enough they felt the change themselves. They first assumed the ship routes had shifted. By the time they had scouted that possibility thoroughly, they’d almost run out of time. They barely scraped together the hundred wretched souls they needed.

“Let’s strike at a different time,” Melusine ventured.

She had only recently joined the Hunters, but she was quick and effective enough that her sisters had acknowledged her skill and invited her to the small council as Guide to the Shoal Leader, Tyna. Queen Nerida lifted her head and measured the young hunter—barely past a century old. Mel tightened her grip on her spear and drew herself up. Tyna looked at her as if she meant to gut her with bare claws, but since Mel had taken the audacity to speak unbidden, she couldn’t afford to shrink now. All for the common goal. All for the greater good. In memory of the Sky Gods.

“What do you mean, at a different time?” the Queen asked.

“If the routes haven’t changed, there are only two possible reasons for the absence of ships, Your Majesty,” Melusine said, growing bolder. “Either the humans have stopped crossing the seas, or they’ve realized we always strike at the same time and are waiting it out until they believe the waters are safe again. If it’s the former, that’s a grave problem. If it’s the latter, then we should simply vary our timing. Sometimes under a waning moon, sometimes waxing. The important thing is that they can’t predict when we’ll surface next.”

She realized she needed air and fell silent, as did the others in the council chamber. Ten merfolk—three representatives from each of the three great hunting Shoals—and the Queen above the Shoal Leaders.

“Suppose you succeed under a waning moon,” the Queen said after a pause that felt endless. “Do we keep the humans here until new moon? Feed them, tend them?”

A few merfolk snickered, but Melusine didn’t laugh. She held the Queen’s cutting, ice-blue gaze.

“I don’t believe that would be a problem for Geynea. We can shift a few chambers and the spare storerooms. They’ll need more food for a time, but as I understand it, we don’t lack for that. It’s worth a trial. In memory of the Sky Gods.”

Her Majesty smiled.

And now it had been a year since Mel had taken Tyna’s place at the head of the Shoal. In the last cycle they had taken the hundred souls from five ships—souls that had fulfilled what they were meant for—and by the look of it, the humans hadn’t found a sudden clever way to avoid them. With luck, they wouldn’t realize for a while yet that as the humans adapted to the attacks, the Hunters adapted to their prey.

When the merchant ship cleared open water, the Shoal fell in behind it. Night, the moon a crescent that grew night by night. Melusine preferred full moons that poured light across the surface, but at least the stars weren’t drowned out. She wished she had more time to simply watch the sky. That luxury wasn’t hers. She had to focus.

Scouting was a crucial element of any raid. Within minutes they’d reported three harpooners and three with thundering firearms. Which meant doubling that number as a precaution. And once they made noise—inevitable—more would flood the deck, likely armed as well.

Melusine swam beneath the keel. Their easiest work came when the wood was old and rotting, or at least neglected. Then it was child’s play to punch a hole, wait for the ship to sink, and gather their victims from the water. Unfortunately, like everything human, this improved year by year. Shipwrights kept finding clever ways to keep their weapons from breaching the bottom or sides, or to make repairs quick and easy before a ship could go down.

No luck this time. The hull had been freshly lacquered; some planks had clearly been replaced. She didn’t give up. Still worth a try.

She opened her mind to the Shoal and swept across them. Fifty merfolk. Fifty sisters waiting on the edge of a command. She picked the five youngest, the ones who hadn’t seen an action yet, to try to breach the hull—an important task, and a safe one. She would carry them home to their mothers. She wouldn’t have to bring the news that they’d fallen on their first hunt.

The young ones arrowed under the ship and set to work, first with spears, then with claws, prying and tearing at the hull. Melusine watched them with brief satisfaction and hoped they would manage.

Waves of impatience rolled over her. Her sisters were ready. More than ready—they thirsted for it. Idle clawed fingers curled and uncurled. Sharp teeth clicked as if they already felt flesh between them, the taste of blood on their tongues. Mel sank into the feelings pouring through her, as earlier she had sunk into the savor of outwitting sailors. Easier that way to hush her own doubts, which seemed to trouble no one else.

She swam to the front of the Shoal, turned to face her sisters, and raised her spear. She stabbed the point toward the ship in one crisp motion. Forty-five merfolk surged for the hull, swarming up along planking, carvings, and hanging ropes.

Melusine fell back for a run-up, then drove forward at full speed, bursting from the waves to catch as high as she could and start climbing for the deck.

A shout. A splash. She glanced back for a heartbeat—just enough to see one of her sisters loop her fin around a thrashing sailor.

One.
